title: Dye Transfer on Cotton
timeEstimate: 20–60 minutes; success depends heavily on whether it's been hot-dried
stainChemistry: Dye transfer is what happens when a color-bleeding garment (new dark denim, red shirt, non-colorfast item) shares a wash cycle with lighter items and the loose dye migrates onto them. The transferred dye bonds to cotton the same way intentional dyeing does — hydrogen bonds, Van der Waals, sometimes ionic — so once heat-set it behaves as if it were part of the fiber's original color. Pre-dryer: mostly recoverable. Post-hot-dryer: often permanent. Cotton tolerates oxygen-side oxidizers and reducing bleaches at home.
whyThisWorks: OxiClean or a dedicated color remover (Rit Color Remover — sodium hydrosulfite reducing bleach) breaks the chromophore. Re-washing immediately in cold water + OxiClean is the strongest first response while dye is still mobile. Color remover is the home nuclear option for whites — don't use on anything you want to keep colorfast. Heat is the absolute enemy; a hot-dryer cycle can turn a recoverable transfer into a permanent one.
defaultAssumption: Assume the user just pulled a load out of the wash and noticed pink / blue / gray on items that shouldn't have any color. The critical decision fork: has it been DRYER'D yet? Pre-dryer = high-confidence DIY; post-dryer = often pro or accept.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Your memory of what just happened
spottingProtocol.instruction: HAS THE LOAD BEEN THROUGH A HOT DRYER YET? If NO: immediate re-wash in cold water + OxiClean is the fix (proceed below). If YES: dye is likely heat-set, home chemistry options are narrower, and escalation may be the honest call. Do NOT dry the affected garments again until treated.

spottingProtocol.dwellTime: 1–4 hours soak, or full wash cycle
spottingProtocol.instruction: Put the dye-transferred garments in a basin of cold water with OxiClean (1 scoop per gallon). Soak 1–4 hours, or run them through a cold wash with OxiClean added. Oxygen bleach breaks the freshly-transferred dye's chromophore. Check at the 1-hour mark.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Rit Color Remover (sodium hydrosulfite) per label
spottingProtocol.instruction: For WHITE cotton only where color remains after OxiClean: Rit Color Remover uses sodium hydrosulfite to reduce the dye. Follow label — ventilation required, product has a sulfur smell. Do NOT use on clothes you want to keep colorfast (reducing bleach strips all dye, including the fabric's own).
spottingProtocol.safetyNote: Sodium hydrosulfite = sulfur smell, respiratory irritant. Ventilate. Gloves. Never mix with chlorine bleach.

spottingProtocol.instruction: For localized dye spots (not whole-garment): Dawn worked in with a soft brush, rinse. Repeat if color persists. Good for freshly-caught transfers on cotton that OxiClean alone didn't clear.
Wash cold per care label. AIR-DRY — do NOT run through a hot dryer until the stain is 100% gone. Heat sets any remaining dye permanently. If any color remains, repeat steps 2–4 before drying.

steps: 1. Time fork: pre-dryer = good odds, post-dryer = often pro.
2. Cold re-wash + OxiClean soak. 1–4 hours or full cycle.
3. Rit Color Remover for stubborn transfer on whites. Sodium hydrosulfite. Ventilate.
5. Cold wash + air-dry. Never hot-dry until fully gone.
products: Rit Color Remover (sodium hydrosulfite reducing bleach — whites only)
professionalProtocol.temperature: cold throughout — heat permanently sets transferred dye
warnings: Heat is the enemy — never dryer-set dye transfer
Sodium hydrosulfite strips ALL dye — not colorfast-safe
Never mix peroxide / OxiClean with chlorine bleach
Rit Color Remover has sulfur odor — ventilate

homeSolutions.agent: Did It Go Through the Dryer? Critical Question
homeSolutions.instruction: This stain has ONE time-critical question: has the load been through a HOT DRYER yet? If NO: you have a real chance to remove it. If YES: heat has set the dye and your options narrow dramatically. Either way, DON'T dry the affected garments again — that makes it permanent.

Fill a basin (or bathtub for a big load) with COLD water. Add 1 scoop of OxiClean Versatile per gallon of water. Submerge the dye-transferred garments. Soak 1–4 hours. OxiClean's oxygen bleach breaks the chromophore in the transferred dye. Check the garments at 1 hour; stir occasionally.

homeSolutions.agent: Rit Color Remover for Whites (If Needed)
homeSolutions.instruction: For WHITE cotton where color remains after OxiClean: Rit Color Remover (available at hardware / craft stores) uses sodium hydrosulfite to reduce and remove dye. Follow the box EXACTLY. Ventilate — it has a sulfur smell. Do NOT use on clothes you want to keep colorfast — it'll strip the fabric's own color too.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Sodium hydrosulfite is a respiratory irritant with a sulfur odor. Open a window. Gloves. Never mix with chlorine bleach.

homeSolutions.instruction: If the transfer is a small spot rather than a whole garment: work a drop of Dawn with a soft toothbrush into the spot, rinse cool. Repeat.
Wash cold per care label. HANG TO DRY — do not put through a dryer until the stain is 100% gone. Inspect between cycles. Any remaining color in a hot dryer = permanent set.

If the dye transfer has already been through a hot dryer cycle, home chemistry may not remove it. Take to a dry cleaner and describe the chain of events. Tell them: "Dye transfer on cotton, from [source garment if known], home-treated with cold water + OxiClean [+ Rit Color Remover if applicable]. [Hot-dried before I noticed / not yet dried]."

steps: 1. CRITICAL: has it been through a hot dryer? Yes = often permanent / pro.
4. Rit Color Remover for whites if OxiClean plateaus (ventilate, sulfur smell).
6. Cold wash + AIR-DRY until stain is fully gone.
products: Rit Color Remover (whites only, nuclear option)
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if dye transfer has already been through a hot dryer and home rounds plateau, or if the fabric is colored and Rit Color Remover would strip its dye.
warnings: HEAT SETS dye transfer permanently — never dryer-set
Rit Color Remover strips ALL dye — not for colorfast fabrics
Never mix OxiClean or peroxide with chlorine bleach
Sodium hydrosulfite has sulfur smell — ventilate

neverDo: NEVER hot-dry dye transfer before treatment — permanent set
Never use Rit Color Remover on clothes you want to keep colorfast
Never layer reducing bleach + oxidizing bleach — toxic
Never skip the air-dry step while color remains
fiberSensitivities: Cotton tolerates OxiClean, Rit Color Remover, Dawn, cold wash
Dyed cotton: Rit Color Remover strips fabric dye — whites only

materialWarnings: Rit Color Remover is whites-only (strips all dye)
Sodium hydrosulfite has sulfur smell — ventilate

escalation.when: Dye transfer has been through a hot dryer cycle, OxiClean + Rit Color Remover haven't cleared it, or the affected garment is colored and the nuclear reducing bleach would strip its fabric dye.
escalation.whatToTell: "Dye transfer on cotton from [source garment], home-treated with cold water + OxiClean [+ Rit Color Remover]. [Hot-dried before treatment / not yet dried]."

contextQuestions: Has the affected garment been through a hot dryer?
Do you know the source (new dark denim, red shirt, unknown)?
Is the affected item white or colored?
How much time has passed since the wash?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Dye transfer is the time-+ heat-critical stain where catching it BEFORE the dryer is the whole protocol. Post-dryer, even pros often can't remove it.
